critique.summary: The article advocates for chest fridges over traditional vertical-door refrigerators, claiming they are more energy-efficient and better at preserving food due to minimized temperature fluctuations. The author shares personal experience with converting a freezer into a fridge and using modern inverter freezers, emphasizing lower energy consumption and reduced peak power demand suitable for off-grid systems.
mainPoints: Vertical-door refrigerators are less efficient because they work against the natural behavior of cold air.
Chest fridges consume significantly less energy, with the author's converted Vestfrost freezer using about 0.1 kWh per day.
Chest fridges maintain more stable temperatures, improving food preservation.
The author has supplied freezer-to-fridge conversion kits for 20 years and notes that some manufacturers now offer chest freezers with temperature control suitable for refrigeration.
Modern inverter-based freezers like CHiQ models offer benefits such as lower energy consumption and reduced peak power demand, making them suitable for off-grid battery systems.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about chest freezers generally being more energy-efficient than upright refrigerators are supported by some energy studies, as chest freezers tend to have better insulation and less cold air loss when opened. The author's reported energy consumption figures for specific models are plausible but anecdotal and would require independent verification. The assertion that most household refrigerators waste at least 1 kWh per day is broadly consistent with typical energy use of older or less efficient models. The benefits of inverter compressors in reducing peak power demand are well documented. However, the article lacks citations to independent studies or multiple perspectives, relying heavily on personal experience and promotion of specific products, which limits its reliability.
considerations: Energy efficiency can vary widely between models and brands of refrigerators and freezers, so generalizations should be made cautiously.
User convenience and accessibility of chest fridges versus upright refrigerators are important factors not discussed.
The environmental impact of manufacturing and disposing of refrigeration equipment is not addressed.
The article does not provide comparative data from third-party testing or recognized energy efficiency certifications.
Potential limitations of chest fridges, such as space usage and ease of organization, are not considered.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the practicality and efficiency of chest fridges compared to traditional vertical refrigerators. Commenters weigh the energy savings and thermal advantages of chest fridges against their usability, space requirements, and convenience.

comments.author: PaulKeeble
comments.text:
Its possible to design internal structures such that its easier to use as a Fridge and freezer with some loss of space to avoid having to reach down into it. It would waste space and some efficiency however, the more complicated it becomes with assisted lifting and such the worse the gap would become. But the problem is often space, a lot of kitchens do not have 2x the floor area to be putting in chests making them good for secondary storage somewhere else but not a primary kitchen appliance.

There is no doubt its better thermally just because cold air falls out the front of a normal fridge/freezer and huge amounts of energy are wasted everytime you open the door. A chest design looses considerably less of its cooled air but its also a lot more awkward to use and ends up less floor space efficient.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced and nuanced view, acknowledging both the thermal efficiency of chest fridges and their usability challenges, supported by logical reasoning.

comments.author: mapontosevenths
comments.text:
I have a bad back and bending over hurts. Statistically it will also start to hurt you someday.

Even if we ignore the pain, there is no way to organize food in a chest freezer effectively. To reach items on the bottom one must remove all the food that sits above it. This wastes time and effort that could better be spent on other things. Meaning the opportunity cost is too high, even if it saves me money on electricity.
comments.analysis: Raises valid ergonomic and organizational concerns about chest fridges, highlighting practical difficulties that affect usability despite potential energy savings.

comments.author: tempestn
comments.text: It's a cool idea, and might be great for a secondary fridge. For a primary fridge though, it's so much more convenient to have direct access to everything through a vertical door. I like energy efficiency, but I'm willing to pay 300kWh a year (around $40 here) for that convenience, let alone the space efficiency.
comments.analysis: Offers a reasonable perspective on the use case of chest fridges, favoring vertical fridges for primary use due to convenience, which aligns with common consumer preferences.

comments.author: zeroq
comments.text: It's more about freezers than fridges. Less frequent access and ton more work to get the temps back. I never thought about it but it was such an a-ha moment for me when I recently learned about it that I'm genuinely flabbergasted why it's not more popular.
comments.analysis: Highlights the difference in usage patterns between freezers and fridges, correctly noting that chest freezers benefit from less frequent access and better temperature retention.

comments.author: Hextinium
comments.text:
This reminds me of the Technology Connections fridge rant video. Similar arguments all around, the dumping effect of cold out of a vertical fridge is pretty crazy to watch with a thermal camera.

https://youtube.com/watch?v=CGAhWgkKlHI
comments.analysis: References external content that supports the argument about energy loss in vertical fridges, reinforcing the discussion with empirical evidence.

comments.author: doughecka
comments.analysis: Provides an interesting anecdote about an innovative fridge design, adding context though not directly addressing the core discussion about chest fridges.

comments.author: erelong
comments.text:
Just have to make it either easy to buy or easy to mod and emphasize energy savings and lots of people would be interested

Edit: looks like a few chest freezers have a "fridge" setting, which sounds like the easiest way to do this for those interested (maybe)
comments.analysis: Suggests practical approaches to increase adoption of chest fridges, noting existing products that partially fulfill this niche.

comments.author: AndrewSwift
comments.text: Drawers would solve this in a vertical fridge.
comments.analysis: Proposes a simple design solution to improve vertical fridge usability, addressing some ergonomic concerns raised in the discussion.

comments.author: anjel
comments.text: Modern refrigerators are designed for browsing. A chest fridge could save a person a lot of calories over time
comments.analysis: Makes an interesting point about user behavior and potential health benefits, though this is more speculative and less central to the main debate.

comments.author: mememememememo
comments.text:
Why?

a: space.

A standup fridge freezer is floor space efficient.

How much rent is the chest freezer using per year :)

Made up numbers 10k for 1000sqft

10 per sq ft

So say $40 a year in rent. Still not too bad I guess
comments.analysis: Focuses on the economic and spatial trade-offs of chest fridges, emphasizing the cost of floor space which is a valid consideration.
